---
title: "Learning from Data"
engine: markdown
---

::: {.lm-hero}
[Chapter 1 · Introduction]{.eyebrow}

# Learning from Data

[Every model earns its keep by beating the simplest guess there is.]{.dek}
:::

Welcome to the interactive companion to *Learning Machines: A Statistical Introduction*. Each chapter pairs the book's exposition with code you can **run in your browser** — Python via Pyodide, R via WebR — or **open in Colab** for the deep-learning chapters. Nothing to install; the code below runs on your own machine, in this page.

::: {.howto}
[How to read this site]{.lbl}

- **Run it yourself.** Code cells run live in your browser — click **Run Code**. The first run downloads the Python (Pyodide) or R (WebR) runtime once; that takes a few seconds, during which the Run button stays grey, then turns red when it is ready.
- **Python or R, your choice.** Every example carries both. Pick a tab and the rest of the page follows; the two are genuine twins, not a translation of one into the other.
- **Same method, sometimes different digits.** When a cell uses fixed data the two languages agree to the last digit; when a cell draws a random sample they differ a little, and that difference is the lesson — what holds is the method, not the digits.
- **Deep learning runs in Colab.** Chapters 9–12 use PyTorch, too heavy for the browser, so they open in Google Colab instead.
:::

Machine learning is the art of turning data into predictions that hold up on cases you have not seen. Before reaching for anything elaborate, it pays to fix a [baseline]{.term}: the simplest model that ignores the inputs entirely and predicts the same number every time. For a numeric target, that number is the mean. Any model worth its complexity must do better than this.

Compute the baseline on a set of Frankfurt sale prices, and the error it leaves behind. Every later chapter is a campaign to shrink that error.

```{=html}
<figure class="lm-chart">
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 340" width="100%" preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMid meet">
  <line x1="0" y1="6" x2="0" y2="30" stroke="#E3120B" stroke-width="6"/>
  <text class="ctitle" x="14" y="18" font-size="15">The simplest model: predict the average</text>
  <text class="csub" x="14" y="36" font-size="12.5">Frankfurt sale price by living area, &euro; thousands</text>
  <line class="grid" x1="72" y1="274" x2="560" y2="274"/><text x="66" y="278" font-size="11" text-anchor="end">200</text>
  <line class="grid" x1="72" y1="222" x2="560" y2="222"/><text x="66" y="226" font-size="11" text-anchor="end">400</text>
  <line class="grid" x1="72" y1="170" x2="560" y2="170"/><text x="66" y="174" font-size="11" text-anchor="end">600</text>
  <line class="grid" x1="72" y1="118" x2="560" y2="118"/><text x="66" y="122" font-size="11" text-anchor="end">800</text>
  <line class="grid" x1="72" y1="66"  x2="560" y2="66"/><text x="66" y="70" font-size="11" text-anchor="end">1,000</text>
  <line class="fit" x1="72" y1="178" x2="560" y2="178" stroke-dasharray="7 4"/>
  <text x="80" y="171" font-size="11" fill="#31417A" font-family="Inconsolata">baseline = mean &asymp; 569</text>
  <g>
    <circle class="pt" cx="98.6" cy="283.4" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="165.2" cy="226.2" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="249.4" cy="205.1" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="194.0" cy="216.8" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="293.8" cy="190.8" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="338.2" cy="167.4" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="382.5" cy="144.0" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="426.9" cy="128.4" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="449.1" cy="112.8" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="482.3" cy="92.0" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="316.0" cy="180.4" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="227.2" cy="214.2" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="271.6" cy="196.0" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="360.3" cy="157.0" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="404.7" cy="138.8" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="438.0" cy="120.6" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="515.4" cy="71.2" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="116.3" cy="258.4" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="149.6" cy="240.2" r="4.4"/><circle class="pt" cx="205.1" cy="219.4" r="4.4"/>
  </g>
  <line class="axis" x1="72" y1="300" x2="560" y2="300"/>
  <line class="axis" x1="72" y1="60" x2="72" y2="300"/>
  <text x="94.2" y="316" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">50</text><text x="205.1" y="316" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">100</text><text x="316.0" y="316" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">150</text><text x="426.9" y="316" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">200</text><text x="537.8" y="316" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">250</text>
  <text x="316" y="333" font-size="11.5" text-anchor="middle" font-style="italic">Living area, m&sup2;</text>
</svg>
<figcaption>Chapter 1's baseline predicts the same average for every house (the dashed line), ignoring living area. Chapter 2 tilts that line to follow the data.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## Python

```{pyodide}
import numpy as np
price = np.array([164,384,465,420,520,610,700,760,820,900,
                  560,430,500,650,720,790,980,260,330,410.])

baseline = price.mean()                       # the simplest model: predict the average
mse = ((price - baseline) ** 2).mean()        # the error it leaves behind

print(f"baseline prediction = {baseline:6.1f}")
print(f"baseline MSE        = {mse:8.1f}")
```

## R

```{webr}
price <- c(164,384,465,420,520,610,700,760,820,900,
           560,430,500,650,720,790,980,260,330,410)

baseline <- mean(price)                       # the simplest model: predict the average
mse <- mean((price - baseline)^2)             # the error it leaves behind

cat(sprintf("baseline prediction = %6.1f\n", baseline))
cat(sprintf("baseline MSE        = %8.1f\n", mse))
```

:::

The mean is the maximum-likelihood prediction under a model with no inputs. It is also, in a sense the book makes precise, the place every regression starts: ordinary least squares (Chapter 2) is what you get when you let the prediction tilt with the features instead of staying flat.

::: {.explore}
[Try it]{.lbl}

What is the smallest model that beats this baseline? Try predicting from a single feature, the living area, and see how much of the variance a straight line can explain. That is exactly where Chapter 2 begins.
:::
